metadata.title: Validate the Name: Trademark Search on Justia
metadata.playlist: Name It & Brand It — Your Strategic Identity
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 6 brief — Brand Strategy + Naming

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 4 of 7. A name you cannot legally use is worthless — and the fastest way to fall in love with a name you cannot use is to skip the trademark check. Part 2B takes your top five names from Episode 3 to trademarks.justia.com, runs each one, screenshots the results, rates the risk, and narrows the field to three. This is not legal advice — it is a first-pass risk screen, and the assignment says to say so. Let's go.

props.segment: Validate the Name: Trademark Search on Justia
props.command: Top 5 names → trademarks.justia.com → screenshot results → risk rating (high/medium/low) → narrow to 3.
props.runningText: not legal advice — a first-pass risk screen; say so explicitly in the submission…
output: 5 names searched on Justia with screenshots
Per name: results summary + potential conflicts + risk rating
3 names that pass the first-pass screen

beats.narration_text: Justia Trademarks is at trademarks.justia.com. It is free to use. In the search bar, type the name you want to check. Search for the exact name first, then the root word without any suffixes. If your name is 'SourceTrace,' search 'SourceTrace' and then 'Source' separately — because a trademark on 'Source' in your product category could create a conflict even if no one has registered the exact compound name. The results page lists trademark registrations and applications, their status — live, dead, cancelled — the class of goods and services, and the registrant. The class matters. A trademark on 'Meridian' in Class 25 (clothing) does not block you from using 'Meridian' in Class 42 (software services). A trademark on 'Meridian' in Class 42 or Class 9 (software) is a potential conflict. Check the class before you assess risk. Justia shows the status of each registration. A dead or cancelled registration is generally not a blocker — verify the status says 'Dead' explicitly. A live registration in the same class as your intended use is the flag to document.

artifactLines: 1. Search the exact name, then the root word alone.
2. Check the class: Class 42 (software services) and Class 9 (software) = your zone.
Class 25 (clothing) does not block a Class 42 registration.
3. Check the status: Live = active; Dead/Cancelled = generally not a blocker.
4. Screenshot every results page — the screenshot is your evidence.
props.sparkLine: Class + status + screenshot. Those three things are the record.

beats.narration_text: For each of the five names, document four things. One: the search process — the exact term you searched, and a screenshot of the results page. Not a description of the screenshot — the actual screenshot, embedded in the PDF or referenced as a labeled appendix. Two: the results summary — how many results appeared, what classes are represented, what the status of each potentially relevant result is. Three: potential conflicts — any live registration in Class 9, 35, or 42 (software, business services, software services) that shares a root word, sounds similar, or covers an overlapping product category. Near-matches count. If there is a live trademark for 'SignalRank' in Class 35 and your tool is called 'Signal Rank' as two words, document it. Four: the risk rating — high, medium, or low — with a one-sentence explanation. High means there is an active trademark in your class for a substantially similar name. Medium means there are near-matches or expired trademarks worth monitoring. Low means the search returned no relevant results in relevant classes. After documenting all five, drop the two highest-risk names. The three that remain go to Episode 5.

props.artifactHeading: 4 items per name — no 'I think it's available'
artifactLines: 1. Search process: exact term searched + screenshot of results page.
2. Results summary: count · classes represented · status of relevant results.
3. Potential conflicts: any live registration in Class 9/35/42 for similar name/root.
4. Risk rating: High (live mark, same class) · Medium (near-matches) · Low (no relevant results).
Near-matches count: 'SignalRank' vs 'Signal Rank' = document it.
props.sparkLine: Screenshots are the evidence. 'I think it's available' is not documentation.

beats.act: the honest aside + narrowing to three
beats.narration_text: The honest aside that belongs in your submission. Trademark searches on Justia are a first-pass risk screen. Justia indexes U.S. federal trademark registrations from the USPTO database, but it does not catch state registrations, common law trademark rights from unregistered use, or foreign registrations. A low-risk result on Justia does not mean the name is legally clear to use. If you plan to actually launch a product under the name, consult a trademark attorney. Write that disclaimer in the submission — it shows you understand the difference between a research task and legal clearance. Then narrow from five to three. Drop the two names with the highest risk ratings. If two names have the same risk level, use your brand judgment from Episodes 1 and 2 — which name fits the positioning better? Which aligns with the pillars? The three surviving names go to Episode 5 for URL availability and the final pick.

artifactLines: Write in the submission: 'This is a first-pass risk screen. Not legal advice.'
Justia covers U.S. federal marks only — not state, common law, or foreign registrations.
Low-risk result ≠ legally clear. Actual launch → consult a trademark attorney.
Drop the 2 highest-risk names. Tie → use brand judgment from Episodes 1–2.
3 survivors → Episode 5 for URL check + final name.
props.sparkLine: Research task ≠ legal clearance. Say so. Then keep your 3.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Trademark Search. Five names to Justia. For each: the search process with a screenshot, the results summary, any potential conflicts in Classes 9, 35, or 42, and a risk rating of high, medium, or low with one explanatory sentence. Near-matches count. Screenshots are the evidence — 'I think it's available' is not documentation. The honest disclaimer goes in the submission: this is a first-pass screen, not legal advice, and not a substitute for an attorney before an actual launch. Drop the two highest-risk names. Three names remain. Episode 5 adds the URL check and picks the final name.

props.artifactTitle: Validate the Name: Trademark Search on Justia

Per name: results summary · conflicts in Class 9/35/42 · risk rating (H/M/L + sentence).
Near-matches count. Screenshots are evidence. No 'I think it's available.'
Disclaimer in submission: first-pass screen, not legal advice.
Drop 2 highest-risk. 3 names → Episode 5.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. After you run the five Justia searches and take your screenshots, use this prompt to build the documentation table. Paste it into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I ran trademark searches on Justia for five names. For each name, I have the following information: search term, number of results, relevant results in software or business services classes, status of each relevant result (live or dead), and any near-matches or root-word matches. Here are my five searches: Name 1: [name]. Search term: [exact term searched]. Results: [count]. Relevant class results: [list]. Status: [live/dead]. Near-matches: [description]. Risk: [your initial assessment]. Then Name 2 through Name 5 in the same format. Build a documentation table with five rows — one per name — and six columns: name, search term, relevant results, status, potential conflicts, and risk rating (high/medium/low with one sentence). Then recommend which two names to drop based on risk, and which three names to carry forward to the URL availability check. Remember that this is a first-pass risk screen only, and include that disclaimer in the output.

props.segment: Validate the Name: Trademark Search on Justia
props.command: 5 names. Per name: search term · results count · relevant class results · status · near-matches. Build table: name · term · relevant results · status · conflicts · risk (H/M/L + sentence). Recommend 2 to drop + 3 to carry forward. Include first-pass disclaimer.
props.runningText: take the screenshots first — the table is only useful if the screenshots are in the PDF too…

beats.narration_text: Validate the Name: Trademark Search on Justia. Liam, in for Bear.
